quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary source of energy for most ocean waves?

options.text: Underwater earthquakes
Wind blowing across the ocean surface
The Moon's gravitational pull
Ocean currents

quiz_questions.explanation: Most ocean waves are generated by wind. As wind blows across the ocean surface, friction transfers energy from the air to the water, creating ripples that can grow into larger waves. The strength, duration, and fetch of the wind determine wave size.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the highest point of a wave called?

options.text: Wavelength
Trough
Crest
Amplitude

quiz_questions.explanation: The crest is the highest point of a wave above the resting water level. The lowest point is called the trough. Wave height is measured as the vertical distance from the trough to the crest.

quiz_questions.question_text: What causes tides on Earth?

options.text: Underwater volcanic eruptions
Gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun on Earth's water
Wind pushing water against the continents
Earth's magnetic field pulling on water

quiz_questions.explanation: Tides are caused primarily by the gravitational pull of the Moon and, to a lesser extent, the Sun on Earth's ocean water. The Moon's gravity creates two tidal bulges—one on the side of Earth facing the Moon and one on the opposite side—resulting in the regular rise and fall of sea level.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many high tides typically occur at most coastal locations in a 24-hour period?
quiz_questions.explanation: Most coastal locations experience two high tides and two low tides approximately every 24 hours and 50 minutes (a semidiurnal tidal pattern). This is because Earth rotates through the two tidal bulges created by the Moon's gravity each day.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to waves as they approach shallow water near the shore?

options.text: They speed up and become taller
They slow down, their wavelength decreases, and their height increases
They maintain the same speed and shape
They speed up and their wavelength increases

quiz_questions.explanation: As waves enter shallow water, friction with the sea floor slows the wave base. The top of the wave continues moving faster than the bottom, causing the wave to "pile up"—its wavelength shortens, its height increases, and it eventually steepens until it breaks.

quiz_questions.question_text: When do spring tides occur?

options.text: During strong coastal storms
When the Moon is at its farthest point from Earth
Only during the spring season
When the Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned during full and new moons

quiz_questions.explanation: Spring tides occur during full and new moons when the Sun, Moon, and Earth are roughly aligned. The combined gravitational pull of the Sun and Moon produces the highest high tides and lowest low tides. The name "spring" refers to the tides "springing forth," not the season.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the wavelength of an ocean wave?

options.text: The depth to which the wave energy extends
The vertical distance from crest to trough
The horizontal distance between two consecutive crests
The time it takes for one wave to pass a point

quiz_questions.explanation: Wavelength is the horizontal distance between two successive wave crests (or troughs). It is one of the key measurements used to describe a wave, along with wave height, period, and frequency. Wavelength varies from centimeters for ripples to hundreds of kilometers for tsunamis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of wave is a tsunami?

options.text: A shallow-water wave generated by seismic activity, landslides, or volcanic eruptions
A wind-generated surface wave
A tidal wave caused by the Moon's gravity
A wave caused by strong ocean currents

quiz_questions.explanation: Tsunamis are shallow-water waves typically generated by underwater earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or submarine landslides. Despite their name being sometimes confused with "tidal waves," they have nothing to do with tides. Tsunamis have extremely long wavelengths and travel at very high speeds across the open ocean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the fetch of a wave?

options.text: The speed at which a wave travels
The height of the wave when it breaks
The depth of water beneath the wave
The distance over which wind blows across open water

quiz_questions.explanation: Fetch is the uninterrupted distance over which wind blows across open water. A longer fetch allows wind to transfer more energy to the water, producing larger waves. This is why oceans produce bigger waves than lakes—they have much greater fetch.

quiz_questions.question_text: When do neap tides produce the smallest tidal range?

options.text: During lunar eclipses
During solar eclipses
During full and new moons
During first and third quarter moons

quiz_questions.explanation: Neap tides occur during the first and third quarter moon phases, when the Sun and Moon are at right angles relative to Earth. Their gravitational forces partially cancel each other out, producing the smallest difference between high and low tides (smallest tidal range).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "wave period"?

options.text: The height of a wave from trough to crest
The time it takes for two successive wave crests to pass a fixed point
The distance a wave travels in one hour
The depth at which wave energy no longer affects the water

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave period is the time interval between the passage of two consecutive wave crests past a fixed point. It is typically measured in seconds. Longer wave periods indicate that the wave carries more energy and has traveled a greater distance from its source.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does "tidal range" mean?

options.text: The vertical difference in water level between high tide and low tide
The distance tidal water travels inland
The number of tidal cycles per year
The geographic area affected by tides

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal range is the vertical difference in height between consecutive high and low tides at a given location. It varies depending on the position of the Moon and Sun, local geography, and seafloor shape. The Bay of Fundy in Canada has the world's largest tidal range, exceeding 16 meters.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "wave refraction"?

options.text: The increase in wave speed in deep water
The breaking of waves on a beach
The reflection of waves off a seawall
The bending of waves as they approach shore at an angle due to varying water depths

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave refraction is the bending of waves as they approach the shore at an angle. The part of the wave in shallower water slows down first while the part in deeper water continues at a higher speed, causing the wave to bend and align more parallel to the shore.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does "swell" refer to in wave terminology?

options.text: The highest wave in a group
Smooth, long-wavelength waves that have traveled far from their source
A sudden increase in sea level from a storm
Waves currently being generated by local wind

quiz_questions.explanation: Swell refers to long-wavelength, organized ocean waves that have traveled far from the storm that generated them. As waves leave the storm area, shorter waves fade and longer waves persist and sort themselves into uniform groups. Swell can travel thousands of kilometers across the ocean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a "semidiurnal" tidal pattern?

options.text: Two roughly equal high tides and two low tides per day
One high tide and one low tide per day
Tides that change only once per month
Tides that occur only during full moons

quiz_questions.explanation: A semidiurnal tidal pattern features two approximately equal high tides and two approximately equal low tides each tidal day (about 24 hours and 50 minutes). This is the most common tidal pattern worldwide, seen along much of the Atlantic coast.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a "diurnal" tidal pattern?

options.text: A pattern with one high tide and one low tide per tidal day
Tides that reverse direction every hour
A pattern with no noticeable tide changes
A pattern with four high tides per day

quiz_questions.explanation: A diurnal tidal pattern has only one high tide and one low tide per tidal day. This pattern is less common and is found in certain areas such as parts of the Gulf of Mexico, the South China Sea, and some high-latitude locations.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is "wave height"?

options.text: The horizontal distance between two crests
The speed at which a wave moves through water
The depth of water beneath a breaking wave
The vertical distance from trough to crest

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave height is the vertical distance measured from the lowest point of the wave (trough) to the highest point (crest). It is determined by wind speed, wind duration, and fetch. Larger waves carry more energy and can cause more significant coastal impacts.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a "storm surge"?

options.text: A rapid increase in ocean salinity during a hurricane
An unusually large wave caused by an earthquake
A lightning strike on the ocean surface
An abnormal rise in sea level caused by strong winds and low pressure during a storm

quiz_questions.explanation: Storm surge is an abnormal rise in sea level above the predicted astronomical tide, caused by strong winds piling water against the coast and low atmospheric pressure during a storm. It is often the most deadly and destructive aspect of hurricanes in coastal areas.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the "wave base"?

options.text: The lowest point of a wave's trough
The depth below which wave energy no longer disturbs the water, approximately half the wavelength
The point where a wave first forms
The bottom of the ocean floor

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave base is the depth below which there is no significant water motion due to surface waves. It occurs at approximately one-half the wavelength. Below this depth, water particles are unaffected by the passing wave. When the ocean floor is shallower than the wave base, the wave "feels" the bottom and begins to change.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a "mixed" tidal pattern?

options.text: Tides that alternate between semidiurnal and diurnal each month
Tides that combine fresh and saltwater influences
Two high and two low tides per day of unequal heights
Tides caused by a mix of wind and gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: A mixed (or mixed semidiurnal) tidal pattern features two high tides and two low tides per day, but successive highs and lows have significantly different heights. This is common along the Pacific coast of North America and results from the tilt of the Moon's orbit relative to the equator.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surfer at the beach notices that waves are arriving at regular intervals of about 12 seconds apart and are very smooth with no whitecaps. What type of waves are these?

options.text: Locally generated wind chop when evaluated systematically
Swell waves that have traveled a long distance from a distant storm
Tidal waves, particularly regarding spaced in
Tsunami waves, particularly regarding about when

quiz_questions.explanation: Smooth, uniformly spaced waves with a long period (12 seconds) and no whitecaps are characteristic of ocean swell. These waves have traveled far from the storm that generated them, during which shorter, steeper waves dissipated, leaving only the organized, long-period waves.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal engineer observes that wave energy is concentrated on rocky headlands while adjacent bays receive less wave energy. What process explains this?

options.text: Wave refraction focusing energy on the headlands
Tidal currents pushing waves toward headlands
Wave diffraction
Storm surge targeting headlands

quiz_questions.explanation: Wave refraction causes waves to bend as they approach the coast due to varying water depths. Waves slow down in the shallower water near headlands, causing wave crests to converge and focus their energy on the protruding headlands. Meanwhile, wave energy spreads out and weakens in the adjacent bays.

quiz_questions.question_text: A family visiting the Bay of Fundy in Canada is amazed to see a 16-meter difference between high and low tide. What combination of factors creates such extreme tidal ranges?

options.text: Strong winds constantly push water into the bay
Only the Moon's gravity acting on the bay
Underground rivers feed the bay during high tide
The funnel shape of the bay amplifies the tidal wave, and resonance matches the natural period of the bay

quiz_questions.explanation: The Bay of Fundy's extreme tidal range results from its funnel-like shape that narrows and shallows inland, concentrating tidal water into a smaller area. Additionally, the bay's length creates a natural resonance that closely matches the tidal period, amplifying the tidal range far beyond normal.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a hurricane, a coastal town experiences a 4-meter storm surge combined with a high spring tide. Why is this combination especially dangerous?

options.text: The storm surge and spring tide cancel each other out
The hurricane winds decrease during spring tides
The storm surge adds to the already elevated spring tide, producing an even higher total water level
Spring tides protect against storm surge by creating offshore currents

quiz_questions.explanation: When a storm surge coincides with a spring high tide, the two effects combine to produce an even higher total water level than either would cause alone. This combination greatly increases the risk of coastal flooding, property damage, and loss of life in low-lying areas.

quiz_questions.question_text: A tsunami crosses the open Pacific Ocean at over 700 km/h, but ships at sea barely notice it. Why is a tsunami undetectable in deep water?

options.text: Ships are too heavy to be moved by tsunamis
In deep water, a tsunami has a very long wavelength and very low wave height, so the surface rise is imperceptible
Tsunamis only exist near the shore
Tsunamis travel underwater and do not reach the surface until near shore

quiz_questions.explanation: In the deep open ocean, a tsunami may have a wavelength of 200+ km but a height of only 1 meter or less. This means the sea surface rises and falls over such a vast distance that ships and even instruments can barely detect the gradual change. The wave only becomes destructive when it enters shallow water and its height increases dramatically.

quiz_questions.question_text: A beach community relies on tidal patterns for clam harvesting. If today is a new moon, what type of tide should the harvesters expect?

options.text: Spring tides with an especially large tidal range
No tides because the Moon is not visible
Extremely small tides because the Moon has no gravity during a new moon
Neap tides with a small tidal range

quiz_questions.explanation: During a new moon, the Sun and Moon are aligned on the same side of Earth, combining their gravitational forces. This produces spring tides with the largest tidal range—highest high tides and lowest low tides—ideal for clam harvesting during the extremely low tides.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer is designing a seawall for a coastal city. She notices that waves approaching the shore are bending to become more parallel to the coastline. What principle explains this behavior?

options.text: Wave refraction caused by waves slowing down in shallower water
The Coriolis effect deflects waves
Wave reflection off nearby structures
Magnetic attraction between water and the shoreline

quiz_questions.explanation: As waves approach the shore at an angle, the portion of the wave in shallower water slows down first while the portion still in deeper water continues at higher speed. This speed difference causes the wave front to bend (refract), gradually aligning more parallel to the coastline.

quiz_questions.question_text: A research station monitors wave conditions and notices three-factor conditions: sustained 50 km/h winds, blowing for 24 hours, over 500 km of open water. Which factor, if increased, would produce the largest increase in wave size?

options.text: Only increasing the salinity of the water
Increasing wind speed would produce the most significant increase in wave size
Only changing the direction of the wind
Only increasing the water temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Wind speed has the greatest influence on wave size. Wave height increases approximately with the square of wind speed, meaning that doubling the wind speed can roughly quadruple wave height. While wind duration and fetch also matter, wind speed is the dominant factor.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marine biologist studying intertidal organisms notices that certain species of barnacles and mussels are found only in a narrow band on coastal rocks. How do tidal patterns explain this zonation?

options.text: Tides have no effect on where organisms live
Organisms are randomly distributed regardless of tides
Regular tidal cycles create distinct zones of varying exposure to air and water, and different organisms are adapted to specific exposure levels
Tidal waves physically push organisms into certain areas

quiz_questions.explanation: The regular rise and fall of tides creates distinct intertidal zones with different periods of air exposure and submersion. Organisms that can tolerate long air exposure (like certain barnacles) live in the upper zone, while those needing more water time live lower. This tidal zonation is fundamental to intertidal ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: After a major earthquake in Chile, Hawaii's Pacific Tsunami Warning Center issues a tsunami watch. The quake struck at 2:00 AM and Hawaii is roughly 10,000 km away. Approximately when should residents expect a possible tsunami arrival?

options.text: Three or more days later
It will never reach Hawaii due to the distance
Approximately 12-14 hours later
Within 30 minutes

quiz_questions.explanation: Tsunamis cross the deep open ocean at speeds of approximately 700-800 km/h. At 10,000 km distance, the wave would take roughly 12-14 hours to reach Hawaii. This travel time allows warning centers to alert coastal populations, which is why the Pacific Tsunami Warning System is so important.
